chunks.title: KHARIF 2024 SEASONAL AGRICULTURAL ADVISORY
chunks.text:
_Source PDF: `kharif_2024.pdf`_
June – November 2024 | All Zones
Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | ICAR | State Agricultural Universities
Document Type: Seasonal Advisory

1. Season Overview
The Southwest Monsoon 2024 is forecast to be near-normal to above-normal (104% of Long Period Average) by India
Meteorological Department. Above-normal rainfall is expected across Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Madhya Pradesh. Below-normal
rainfall risk exists in parts of Rajasthan and Gujarat. Farmers should plan crop choices based on their specific district forecasts.
Key message: Good monsoon forecasts support expansion of rice, soybean, and cotton in central India. Groundnut areas in Tamil
Nadu should plan for moisture conservation. Rajasthan farmers should focus on drought-tolerant bajra and cluster bean varieties.

October 2024 – April 2025 | All Zones
Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | ICAR | State Agricultural Universities
Document Type: Seasonal Advisory

1. Season Overview
Rabi 2024-25 begins on the back of a near-normal Kharif season. Soil moisture levels are adequate across most wheat-growing
states — Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and Madhya Pradesh. Reservoir storage is above average, supporting assured irrigation
for wheat and mustard. Cold wave risk in December–January must be monitored for mustard and cumin.
Key message: Wheat MSP has been increased to ■2,275/qtl — excellent returns expected. Mustard prices remain strong due to
edible oil demand. Onion Rabi planting window is October–November for February–March harvest.

Regional Notes
Rainfall Pattern

Kerala receives over 3000 mm of annual rainfall from two monsoons — Southwest (June–September) and Northeast
(October–November). Dry season is December–May. Thrissur district averages 2800–3200 mm annually. Excess rain
causes waterlogging and fungal diseases. Drainage management is as important as irrigation.

chunks.title: FARMWISE AI — CROP CALENDAR
chunks.text:
Common Challenges
Phytophthora foot rot (quick wilt) in black pepper is a major economic threat — treated with Bordeaux mixture. Coconut root
wilt disease is endemic in certain areas. Rhinoceros beetle damages young coconut palms. Banana bunchy top virus
spreads through aphids — use virus-free planting material. Rubber requires 5–7 years before tapping begins.

Intercropping Recommendations
Coconut + Black Pepper (grown up coconut palm as standard). Coconut + Banana + Cocoa in multi-tier system. Coconut +
Pineapple ground cover. Rubber + Cocoa in young rubber plantations (first 5 years). Tapioca + Cowpea in mixed planting.

Organic Farming Advisory
Kerala promotes organic farming for spices and coconut. Use Trichoderma viride for soil treatment. Green manure crops like
sun hemp improve soil health. Vermicompost application recommended for all crops. Neem cake (2 kg/palm) annually for
coconut improves resistance to root wilt.

Regional Notes
Rainfall Pattern
Nashik and Deccan Plateau receive 600–800 mm rainfall annually from the Southwest Monsoon (June–September). Rain is
concentrated in July–August. October onwards is dry. Borewell irrigation is essential for Rabi season. Black cotton soil
retains moisture well but cracks in dry season.

Common Challenges
Late blight in tomato during October–November when humidity rises. Thrips infestation in onion during dry Rabi season.
Wheat rust (yellow and brown) in February–March when temperatures rise. Water scarcity in March–May affects summer
crops. Borewell dependency increases cost of cultivation.

Intercropping Recommendations
Tomato + Basil repels whitefly and improves aroma. Onion + Carrot improves land utilization in Rabi. Wheat + Chickpea at
4:1 ratio for Rabi season. Marigold as border crop around tomato to trap fruit borer.

Market Notes
Nashik is India's largest onion market. Onion prices are highly volatile — monitor APMC Lasalgaon daily. Tomato prices peak
in January–February. Wheat procurement at MSP through FCI is available. Plan harvest timing to avoid market glut in
October for Kharif onion.

Regional Notes
Rainfall Pattern
Punjab receives 600–900 mm annually, mostly from the Southwest Monsoon (July–September). Winters are cold and foggy
(December–January) which impacts wheat tillering. Summer is very hot (May–June). Canal irrigation from Beas and Sutlej
rivers is well developed. Groundwater is at risk of depletion from paddy cultivation.

Common Challenges
Yellow rust is the most serious wheat disease in Punjab — monitor from January. Rice stubble burning causes air pollution
and depletes soil organic matter. Brown plant hopper in rice has developed insecticide resistance — use IPM. Waterlogging
in low-lying areas during heavy monsoon. High water use in paddy — Punjab Government promotes Direct Seeded Rice
(DSR).

chunks.title: FARMWISE AI — CROP CALENDAR
chunks.text:
Intercropping Recommendations
Wheat + Mustard border rows (6:1) in Rabi. Maize + Cowpea (3:1) improves soil nitrogen. Basmati rice is grown as mono
crop — do not intercrop. Consider maize-wheat rotation instead of rice-wheat to reduce water use.

Water Advisory
Punjab State Government offers subsidy for drip and sprinkler irrigation. Direct Seeded Rice (DSR) saves 15–20% water vs
transplanted paddy. Avoid paddy sowing before June 10 (state law) to reduce groundwater extraction. Laser land leveling
improves irrigation efficiency by 20%.

chunks.title: FARMWISE AI — CROP CALENDAR
chunks.text:
Regional Notes
Rainfall Pattern
Rajasthan receives only 200–400 mm of annual rainfall, concentrated in July–August from the Southwest Monsoon. Western
Rajasthan (Jodhpur, Barmer) receives less than 200 mm. Jaipur district averages 550 mm. Hot dry winds (loo) in May–June
cause crop damage. Fog in December–January affects mustard and cumin.

Common Challenges
Water scarcity is the primary constraint — every crop choice must factor water availability. Downy mildew in bajra during
humid spells after rain. Aphids in mustard are the most serious pest — they can cause 40–50% yield loss if uncontrolled.
Cumin wilt (Fusarium) spreads in waterlogged conditions — never over-irrigate cumin. Sandy soil has low nutrient retention
— apply organic matter regularly.

Intercropping Recommendations
Bajra + Moth Bean (2:1) — traditional and drought-tolerant system. Mustard + Chickpea (2:1) in Rabi for risk management.
Cumin is always grown alone — intercropping reduces essential oil quality. Cluster bean + Bajra mixed cropping for fodder
and grain.

chunks.title: FARMWISE AI — CROP CALENDAR
chunks.text:
Water Conservation Advisory
Rajasthan Government provides drip irrigation subsidy up to 70% for horticulture crops. Rainwater harvesting through tanka
and kund systems recommended. Micro watershed structures help recharge groundwater. Indira Gandhi Canal commands
area in western Rajasthan — farmers here can grow more water-intensive crops. Mulching with crop residue reduces
evaporation by 30%.

Common Challenges

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Bajra is the primary dryland cereal of Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Haryana. It is highly drought-tolerant but susceptible to downy mildew and ergot under specific conditions. It is the staple food crop for millions of small farmers in arid regions where no other cereal can be reliably grown.

---

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
Downy Mildew (Green Ear Disease — Sclerospora graminicola)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- Systemic infection — plants stunted and pale from emergence
- White downy growth on lower leaf surface
- Panicle (ear) converted into leaf-like structures (green ear) — no grain formed
- Infected plants are called witches broom — multiple tillers, none forming grain
- Oospores in soil persist for 10+ years

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Cool humid conditions at germination (below 30°C, high humidity)
- Season: Early Kharif — June to July
- Waterlogged or poorly drained soils
- Seed-borne and soil-borne

**Treatment:**
- No field treatment for systemically infected plants — rogue and destroy
- Metalaxyl 35% WS seed treatment — 6 g/kg seed prevents seed-borne infection

**Organic Alternative:**
- Hot water seed treatment — 55°C for 10 minutes
- Use downy mildew-resistant varieties

**Prevention:**
- Use certified seed from downy mildew-free areas
- Metalaxyl seed treatment mandatory in endemic areas
- Remove green ear plants before sporulation
- Crop rotation — avoid pearl millet after pearl millet

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
Downy Mildew (Green Ear Disease — Sclerospora graminicola)

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Honeydew (sticky liquid) oozing from florets at flowering stage
- Honeydew is the first sign — attracts flies and bees
- Honeydew replaced by hard dark purple-brown sclerotia (ergot bodies)
- Ergot bodies replace individual grains in panicle
- Ergot contains toxic alkaloids — grain is unfit for human or animal consumption

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Cool humid conditions at flowering (below 20°C, high humidity)
- High altitude bajra fields (above 500 m) in Rajasthan
- Season: August when early-sown bajra flowers during cooler humid conditions

**Treatment:**
- No chemical treatment — prevention only
- Remove and destroy infected panicles before sclerotia mature

**Organic Alternative:**
- Wash panicles with water spray to reduce honeydew and spore spread

**Prevention:**
- Sow recommended varieties on time — avoid early sowing in high-altitude areas
- Use ergot-resistant or tolerant varieties
- Do not use infected grain as seed
- Immerse seed in 10% salt solution — ergot bodies float and can be removed

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Diptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Dead heart in seedlings — central shoot pulls out easily with foul smell
- Yellowing and wilting of central leaf before death
- Maggots visible inside killed shoot
- Severely attacked fields require re-sowing
- Economic threshold: 10% dead hearts

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C
- Humid conditions at seedling stage
- Season: Early Kharif — June to July
- Late-sown bajra escapes shoot fly as pest population declines

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorpyrifos 20% EC — 2 ml/litre foliar spray at 10–15 DAS
- Alternatively: Thiamethoxam 25% WG — 0.3 g/litre spray

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem seed kernel extract 5% spray
- Seed treatment with Imidacloprid 600 FS — 5 ml/kg

**Prevention:**
- Timely sowing — June 15 to July 15 is optimum
- Seed treatment with Imidacloprid 600 FS
- Use resistant varieties (MPMH-17, HHB-67 Improved)
- Destroy crop debris from previous season

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Bajra (Pearl Millet) Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Lepidoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Dead heart at vegetative stage — similar to shoot fly but older plants
- Pinholes on leaves from early larval feeding
- Frass visible in whorl
- Late infestation causes chaffy panicles and broken stems

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C
- Season: July to August — after shoot fly peak
- Humid conditions

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Fipronil 0.3% GR — 7.5 kg/ha applied in whorl
- Alternatively: Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC — 0.3 ml/litre spray

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichogramma chilonis egg cards — 5 cards per acre
- Bacillus thuringiensis spray — 2 g/litre

**Prevention:**
- Early sowing to avoid peak borer season
- Light traps to catch adult moths
- Intercrop with cowpea which harbors natural enemies

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Coconut is Kerala's most important crop and a major source of livelihood for millions of farmers in coastal India. Root wilt disease is an endemic and incurable problem in certain zones of Kerala, while rhinoceros beetle and red palm weevil cause serious damage to palms across India. Integrated management combining regular monitoring, cultural practices, and targeted treatments is essential.

---

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Phytoplasma (Mycoplasma-like organism)

**Symptoms:**
- Leaf flaccidity — leaves hang down losing their normal erect posture
- Yellowing starts from outer older leaves and progresses inward
- Root necrosis — feeder roots turn brown and die
- Reduced nut production; nuts smaller with less kernel
- Eventual death of palm over years
- No cure exists — incurable endemic disease in certain zones of Kerala

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Endemic in certain areas of Kerala (Kuttanad, Kochi region)
- Spreads through planthoppers (Proutista moesta) which transmit phytoplasma
- Poorly drained, waterlogged soils worsen symptoms
- Any season; progressive disease

**Treatment:**
- No cure — palliative care to extend productive life of affected palms
- Inject micronutrient mixture (ferrous sulfate, zinc sulfate, manganese sulfate) into trunk
- Drench root zone with dilute NPK fertilizer
- Keep affected palms well-nourished to maintain partial productivity

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Organic Alternative:**
- Organic matter application improves root health and slows progression
- Vermicompost 25 kg per palm annually

**Prevention:**
- Plant certified Root Wilt-resistant varieties in endemic areas
- Control planthopper vector with Imidacloprid drench
- Improve drainage in root zone
- Do not plant coconut in areas with known root wilt history

---

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- Yellowing and wilting of innermost spear leaf (spindle)
- Spindle pulls out easily — base shows dark brown rotting with foul smell
- Rotting progresses down into growing point (bud) — killing the palm
- Coconut has a single growing point — once bud dies, palm cannot recover
- Outer leaves remain green initially making early detection difficult

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Heavy rainfall during monsoon — especially June–August
- Young palms (1–5 years) most vulnerable
- Waterlogged poorly-drained soils
- High humidity with poor air circulation in dense plantings

**Treatment:**
- Remove rotted spindle and bud tissue completely — clean with knife
- Fill cavity with Bordeaux paste (copper sulfate + lime)
- Pour Copper oxychloride 3 g/litre solution into bud cavity
- For early-stage: Metalaxyl drench at vine base

**Organic Alternative:**
- Bordeaux paste applied to exposed tissue after removal of infected bud
- Trichoderma application to root zone as preventive

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Drain excess water from root zone
- Remove and destroy infected spindles immediately
- Spray Bordeaux mixture 1% on crown and collar before monsoon
- Avoid injuries to palm — beetles create entry points for Phytophthora

---

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Coleoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- V-shaped or rectangular cuts on fronds — characteristic feeding damage
- Holes bored into soft central shoot (crown)
- Cut fronds hang like flag leaves
- Bud damage causes deformed leaves with serrated edges
- Severe crown boring kills the growing point and eventually the palm
- Grubs breed in decaying organic matter — compost heaps, log piles

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Adults most active at night — breed in decaying wood and compost
- New plantings near decaying coconut logs are at high risk
- Season: Year-round in tropical humid climate

**Treatment:**
- Remove adult beetles from crown — use hooked wire inserted into bore hole
- Apply Naphthalene balls (3–4 per crown) to repel adults
- Carbaryl 5% dust — 25 g applied into bore hole

**Organic Alternative:**
- Oryctes baculovirus (Nudivirus) biocontrol — mix in breeding sites
- Apply to all identified breeding sites
- Metarhizium anisopliae fungus application in compost heaps

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Remove and burn all decaying organic matter — coconut stumps, compost heaps
- Do not allow coconut logs to decompose near garden
- Install pheromone traps — 1 per 5 acres to mass-trap adults
- Inject Neem oil into bore holes

---

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Coleoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Large bore holes in trunk near crown with brown fibrous material extruding
- Fermented odor from bore site
- Wilting and yellowing of crown leaves
- Tunneling damage throughout trunk interior
- Palm dies once the central growing point is reached
- Grubs (large white C-shaped) found inside trunk — up to 60 per palm

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Entry through wounds — beetle attack follows rhinoceros beetle damage, pruning cuts, or injuries
- Any season; year-round in coastal humid areas
- Pheromone attractant produced by infested palms draws more beetles

**Treatment:**
- Inject Chlorpyrifos 20% EC — 5 ml diluted in 100 ml water into bore holes
- Apply cotton wicks soaked in insecticide into holes and seal with clay
- For early detection: insert probe into soft crown area to check for tunneling

**Organic Alternative:**
- Entomopathogenic nematodes (Steinernema carpocapsae) injected into bore holes
- Pheromone traps for mass trapping adults

chunks.title: Coconut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Prevent all trunk injuries — rhinoceros beetle wounds are primary entry points
- Install pheromone traps — Ferrolure+ pheromone lure, 1 trap per 5 acres
- Apply Carbaryl paste on all pruning cuts and wounds immediately
- Remove severely infested palms completely — burn trunk on site
- Regular monitoring — inspect crown monthly

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Groundnut (peanut) is a major oilseed and cash crop in Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Rajasthan. It is particularly vulnerable to Tikka leaf spot and collar rot in humid conditions. Aflatoxin contamination from Aspergillus infection in storage is a serious food safety concern that must be prevented through proper drying and storage.

---

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Tikka Disease — Early and Late Leaf Spot (Cercospora arachidicola & Phaeoisariopsis personata)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Early leaf spot: Circular brown spots with yellow halo on upper leaf surface; white/gray growth on underside
- Late leaf spot: Dark brown to black circular spots, no yellow halo; more numerous
- Severely infected leaves drop prematurely causing defoliation
- Defoliation reduces pod fill significantly — 50–70% yield loss if uncontrolled
- Both diseases appear together; late leaf spot is more aggressive

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–30°C
- Humidity: Above 70%; prolonged leaf wetness
- Season: Kharif — August to September peak

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorothalonil 75% WP — 2 g/litre spray every 10 days from 30 DAS
- Alternatively: Mancozeb 75% WP — 2.5 g/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray — begin preventively at 30 DAS

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre spray
- Sulfur 80% WP — 3 g/litre spray

**Prevention:**
- Use resistant varieties (TAG-24, ICGV-86031)
- Apply gypsum 500 kg/ha at pegging to improve calcium nutrition
- Maintain crop rotation — avoid groundnut after groundnut
- Remove and plow under crop debris

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Tikka Disease — Early and Late Leaf Spot (Cercospora arachidicola & Phaeoisariopsis personata)

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Sudden wilting of seedlings at collar region (soil surface)
- Blackening and rotting of stem at soil level
- Black sclerotia visible on rotted tissue
- Seedlings pull out easily — roots completely rotted
- Patches of dead seedlings in field

**Triggering Conditions:**
- High soil moisture immediately after sowing
- Poor drainage; heavy clay soils
- Seed sown too deep or seed coat damaged
- Season: Early Kharif after heavy rains

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Carbendazim drench at 1 g/litre around affected plants
- Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichoderma viride seed treatment — 4 g/kg seed before sowing

**Prevention:**
- Seed treatment with Thiram 75% WS + Carbendazim 50% WP — 3 g/kg seed
- Ensure good drainage in field
- Avoid over-irrigation at germination stage
- Sow at optimum depth (5 cm) — not too shallow or deep

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect

**Symptoms:**
- Clusters of small black aphids on growing tips and young leaves
- Curling and puckering of young leaves
- Stunted shoot growth
- Sticky honeydew causing sooty mold
- Aphids transmit Groundnut Rosette Virus — most damaging viral disease of groundnut in Africa

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 20–30°C
- Season: Early Kharif — June to July peak when crop is young
- Dry weather with moderate temperature
- Dense planting increases aphid buildup

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL — 0.5 ml/litre spray
- Application method: Foliar spray on growing tips; use only when threshold crossed (50 aphids per plant)

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre spray every 5 days
- Soap solution 10 ml/litre to suffocate colonies

**Prevention:**
- Seed treatment with Imidacloprid 600 FS — 5 ml/kg seed
- Maintain plant vigor through balanced fertilization
- Avoid planting near older infected crops

chunks.title: Groundnut Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Coleoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Patches of wilted plants — pull out easily with no roots attached
- Creamy-white C-shaped grubs found in soil around roots
- Roots and peg zone completely eaten
- Pods show feeding damage — holes and missing sections
- Problem is underground and not visible until severe damage done

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Sandy soils with low clay content
- Season: Kharif — grubs active June to October
- Adult beetles emerge during May–June rains and lay eggs in soil

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorpyrifos 20% EC — 5 litre/ha in irrigation water as drench
- Alternatively: Phorate 10G — 10 kg/ha applied in furrow at sowing

**Organic Alternative:**
- Entomopathogenic nematodes (Heterorhabditis spp.) — soil application
- Metarhizium anisopliae fungus — soil application 2.5 kg/ha

**Prevention:**
- Deep summer plowing to expose and kill grubs and pupae
- Light traps to mass-catch adult beetles during May–June emergence
- Seed treatment with Imidacloprid or Chlorpyrifos
- Avoid applying fresh farmyard manure which attracts egg-laying beetles

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Maize is grown as a Kharif crop in Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Punjab and as a spring crop in Punjab and UP. The introduction of Fall Armyworm (Spodoptera frugiperda) in 2018 has become a major new threat to maize cultivation across India. Early detection and management of this pest is now a top priority.

---

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Lepidoptera) — Invasive Species

**Symptoms:**
- Window-pane feeding on young leaves — transparent patches
- Ragged leaf damage with sawdust-like frass in whorl
- Pinholes on leaves arranged in rows
- In severe cases, entire whorl is consumed — dead heart at vegetative stage
- Caterpillar has inverted Y mark on head; four square spots on abdomen tip

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C
- Season: Active throughout Kharif; peak at whorl stage (V4–V8)
- Migrates over long distances — entire regions affected simultaneously
- Highly resistant to many pesticides — rotate chemicals

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorantraniliprole (Coragen) 18.5% SC — 0.3 ml/litre into whorl
- Alternatively: Spinetoram 11.7% SC — 0.9 ml/litre
- Application method: Apply directly into whorl using knapsack — cover all plants

**Organic Alternative:**
- Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki — 2 g/litre into whorl at early larval stage
- NSKE 5% spray every 5 days
- Release Trichogramma pretiosum egg parasitoid cards

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Monitor from emergence — check 20 plants per acre twice weekly
- Install pheromone traps for Fall Armyworm — 5 per acre
- Early planting to avoid peak moth migration period
- Encourage natural enemies — do not spray broad-spectrum insecticides

---

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Viral

**Symptoms:**
- Bright yellow to white streaks running parallel to leaf veins
- Young infected plants are severely stunted
- No grain set in infected plants
- Transmitted by leafhoppers (Cicadulina spp.)
- No cure once infected

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Leafhopper populations high in warm dry conditions
- Season: Early Kharif when leafhoppers are active
- Young plants (V1–V6) are most susceptible

**Treatment:**
- No treatment — infected plants must be removed and destroyed
- Control leafhopper vector with Imidacloprid 17.8% SL — 0.5 ml/litre

**Organic Alternative:**
- Reflective silver mulch repels leafhoppers
- Neem oil spray on young plants to deter leafhoppers

**Prevention:**
- Use resistant hybrid varieties
- Seed treatment with Imidacloprid to protect against early leafhopper feeding
- Roguing and destroying infected plants immediately

---

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Lower internodes soften and rot — stalk collapses
- Internal stalk tissue shows pinkish or brown discoloration
- Plant lodges before maturity — ear contacts ground and rots
- Roots become brown and necrotic
- Premature ripening with poor grain fill

**Triggering Conditions:**
- High nitrogen, low potassium nutrition
- Water stress followed by heavy rain at tasseling
- Season: August–September in Kharif
- Dense planting reduces air circulation

**Treatment:**
- No effective field treatment — focus on prevention
- Harvest early if stalk rot detected to minimize loss

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichoderma viride seed treatment
- Balanced potassium application reduces susceptibility

**Prevention:**
- Balanced NPK — do not skip potassium
- Avoid water stress at tasseling and silking
- Plant at recommended spacing — do not crowd
- Use stalk rot resistant hybrids
- Harvest promptly at maturity — delayed harvest worsens loss

chunks.title: Maize Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- White downy growth on underside of lower leaves
- Pale green to yellow striping on upper leaf surface
- Severely affected plants are stunted with narrow twisted leaves
- No ears formed — or small deformed ears
- Systemic infection — entire plant affected from seed or early infection

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Cool humid conditions
- High rainfall at germination stage
- Season: Early Kharif — June to July

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Metalaxyl 35% WS seed treatment — 6 g/kg seed before sowing
- Foliar spray with Metalaxyl + Mancozeb — 2.5 g/litre at first symptom

**Organic Alternative:**
- No effective organic treatment at advanced stage
- Remove and destroy infected plants before sporulation

**Prevention:**
- Seed treatment with Metalaxyl is essential in downy mildew-prone areas
- Use resistant hybrids
- Avoid planting in waterlogged or low-lying areas
- Crop rotation with non-host crops (legumes, cotton)

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Mustard is the primary oilseed Rabi crop of Rajasthan, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh, and Uttar Pradesh. Aphids are the most economically damaging pest, capable of causing 40–60% yield loss in severe infestations. Timely monitoring and action at the economic threshold level is critical for profitable mustard cultivation.

---

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Circular brown to dark brown spots with concentric rings on leaves
- Yellow halo around spots; older spots merge to form large blighted areas
- Dark elongated lesions on stems and branches
- Silique (pod) infection causes premature shattering and seed discoloration
- Defoliation under severe infection

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 20–28°C
- Humidity: Above 75%; dew and fog in Rabi season
- Season: January to March in Rajasthan, Haryana

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP — 2 g/litre spray every 10 days
- Alternatively: Iprodione 50% WP — 1.5 g/litre at pod formation stage

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper oxychloride 3 g/litre spray
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre fortnightly

**Prevention:**
- Use certified disease-free seed
- Seed treatment with Thiram 75% WS — 3 g/kg seed
- Maintain spacing of 45×15 cm for air circulation
- Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip or furrow

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- White blister-like pustules on underside of leaves, stems, and inflorescences
- Upper leaf surface shows yellow patches opposite pustules
- Infected flower stalks become distorted and swollen (staghead)
- Staghead causes complete failure of pod formation
- Can cause 30–40% yield loss in severe years

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 10–18°C — cool weather
- Humidity: High; fog and dew in December–January
- Season: December to February

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb — 2.5 g/litre spray at first sign
- Alternatively: Ridomil Gold 2.5 g/litre

**Organic Alternative:**
- Bordeaux mixture 1% spray
- Remove and destroy infected flower stalks immediately

**Prevention:**
- Use white rust tolerant varieties (Pusa Bold, Kranti)
- Timely sowing — October 1–15
- Crop rotation with non-crucifer crops

---

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect

**Symptoms:**
- Dense colonies of mustard-green aphids on growing tips, flowers, and pods
- Leaves and shoots curl inward and distort
- Honeydew causes sooty mold
- Flowering and pod set severely disrupted — main yield loss cause
- Economic threshold: 20–25 aphids per plant at vegetative; 46–50 per plant at flowering

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 15–25°C; cold dry January conditions are ideal
- Season: January to February — peak in cold dry spells
- Populations can increase 10x in a week during favorable conditions

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Dimethoate 30% EC (Rogor) — 2 ml/litre spray at economic threshold
- Alternatively: Oxydemeton methyl (Metasystox) — 1.5 ml/litre
- Application method: Spray in morning; cover all plant parts including flower clusters

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre + soap 2 ml/litre spray
- Spray high-pressure water jet to dislodge colonies
- Protect natural enemies — syrphid flies, ladybirds, lacewings

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Timely sowing reduces aphid exposure period
- Avoid late sowing which extends crop into peak aphid season
- Monitor twice weekly from December
- Attract natural enemies by maintaining habitat diversity

---

chunks.title: Mustard Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Hymenoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Caterpillar-like larvae (slate-gray with orange-yellow tinge) feeding on leaves
- Leaves skeletonized — only veins remain
- Severe attack can defoliate entire field rapidly
- Adults are yellow and black wasp-like insects
- Early stage (October–November) attack on seedlings most damaging

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Season: October to November after sowing; second generation in February
- Cool moist conditions after first rain following sowing
- More severe in late-sown crops

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Quinalphos 25% EC — 2 ml/litre spray
- Alternatively: Malathion 50% EC — 2 ml/litre

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre spray
- Hand collection of larvae in small fields
- Spray in evening when larvae are active

**Prevention:**
- Timely sowing — October 1–15
- Early sowing escapes first generation peak
- Deep plowing to destroy pupae in soil
- Maintain field hygiene

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Onion is a high-value commercial crop grown extensively in Maharashtra (Nashik), Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Madhya Pradesh. It is highly sensitive to thrips and fungal diseases during bulb development. The crop requires careful monitoring as late-stage disease or pest damage directly impacts market quality and storability.

---

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Small white lesions with purple center on leaves — turns into large papery patches
- Lesions have water-soaked margins, eventually girdling the leaf
- Infected leaves collapse and dry — reducing photosynthesis and bulb fill
- Disease progresses from older outer leaves inward
- Infected seed stalks break at lesion sites

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 21–30°C
- Humidity: Above 80%; dew and fog favor infection
- Season: Kharif October–November; Rabi February–March
- Wounded leaves from thrips feeding are primary entry points

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP — 2 g/litre spray every 7 days
- Alternatively: Iprodione 50% WP — 1.5 g/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray with sticker (Triton X-100 0.1%) on waxy leaves

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper oxychloride 3 g/litre spray
- Garlic extract 5% spray has antifungal properties

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Use certified disease-free seed or treated seed
- Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip
- Maintain spacing of 10×10 cm for air circulation
- Rotate with non-allium crops every 2–3 years
- Apply sticker to improve spray adhesion on waxy leaf surface

---

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Thysanoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Silver streaks and white patches on inner leaves (feeding scars)
- Leaves curl inward; tips dry and turn brown
- Tiny yellow insects visible with magnification inside leaf whorls
- Severely infested plants are stunted with malformed bulbs
- Thrips transmit Iris Yellow Spot Virus (IYSV)
- Economic threshold: 15–20 thrips per plant

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C; hot dry weather causes population explosion
- Season: Rabi October–March peak; Kharif also significant
- Dry dusty conditions favor thrips
- Dense planting traps heat and creates microhabitat

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Spinosad 45% SC — 0.3 ml/litre spray targeting leaf base
- Alternatively: Fipronil 5% SC — 1.5 ml/litre; rotate with spinosad to prevent resistance
- Application method: Spray into leaf whorl and base — not just on leaf surface

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre + soap 2 ml/litre — spray every 5 days
- Blue sticky traps — 10 per acre to monitor and mass-trap
- Spray in morning or evening when thrips are less active

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Irrigate frequently — thrips dislike wet conditions
- Use reflective silver mulch to disorient thrips
- Avoid planting near older infested crops
- Remove and destroy crop debris immediately after harvest
- Plant marigold border rows as trap crop

---

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- Pale green to yellow patches on outer leaves, covered with violet-gray fuzzy growth in morning
- Infected leaves collapse and dry from tip downward
- Plants in affected patches appear pale and water-stressed
- Bulbs from infected plants store poorly and rot quickly

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 13–25°C (cool humid conditions)
- Humidity: Very high; dew critical for spore germination
- Season: November–February Rabi season during foggy cool nights

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) — 2.5 g/litre spray
- Application method: Foliar spray every 10 days; use sticker for adhesion on waxy leaves

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper-based fungicides (Bordeaux 1%) spray every 7–10 days
- Improve drainage and air circulation

**Prevention:**
- Use disease-free seed and sets
- Adequate spacing — 15×10 cm minimum
- Avoid evening irrigation which promotes overnight leaf wetness
- Crop rotation with non-allium crops

chunks.title: Onion Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Basal Rot (Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cepae)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Plants wilt progressively despite adequate moisture
- Outer leaves yellow from tips downward
- Pinkish-red rot at base of bulb (basal plate)
- Roots show brown discoloration and rot
- Infected bulbs collapse in storage — entire storage lot can be lost

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Soil temperature: 20–28°C
- Favored by waterlogged soils and poor drainage
- Season: Any season but worse in Kharif due to heavy rain
- Spreads in soil — persists for 8–10 years

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Carbendazim 50% WP — drench at 1 g/litre around affected plants
- No effective cure for advanced infection — remove affected plants

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichoderma viride — soil application 2.5 kg/ha at transplanting
- Pseudomonas fluorescens drench around plant base

**Prevention:**
- Seed and set treatment with Thiram 75% WS — 3 g/kg
- Raise transplants in sterilized soil
- Improve field drainage — ridge and furrow planting
- Crop rotation — avoid allium crops for 3–4 years in infected field
- Avoid waterlogging at any growth stage

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Black pepper (Piper nigrum) is a high-value spice crop grown primarily in Kerala, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu. Phytophthora quick wilt is the most devastating disease — it can kill entire vines within weeks and has caused massive losses in Kerala's pepper gardens. Integrated disease management combining cultural practices, biocontrol, and judicious use of fungicides is essential.

---

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
Phytophthora Foot Rot / Quick Wilt (Phytophthora capsici)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- Sudden wilting of entire vine within days to weeks — "quick wilt"
- Water-soaked lesions at collar region (base of vine near soil)
- Dark brown to black rotting of roots and basal stem
- Leaves turn yellow and fall rapidly — bare vine
- Berries blacken and fall prematurely
- Soil splash during rain spreads spores to leaves — brown lesions on leaves and spikes

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Heavy continuous rainfall — especially July–September monsoon
- Poorly drained, waterlogged soils
- Temperature: 20–30°C
- Disease spreads rapidly through field during monsoon via irrigation and runoff

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) — 2.5 g/litre soil drench around vine base
- Foliar spray of Bordeaux mixture 1% on leaves and spikes
- Copper hydroxide (Kocide) 3 g/litre as drench and spray

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichoderma harzianum — 50 g per vine as soil application before monsoon
- Pseudomonas fluorescens — 20 g per vine soil application
- Bordeaux mixture 1% preventive spray before monsoon begins

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
Phytophthora Foot Rot / Quick Wilt (Phytophthora capsici)
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Ensure excellent drainage in pepper garden — do not allow waterlogging ever
- Apply Bordeaux mixture 1% spray in May (before monsoon) as preventive
- Remove and destroy badly infected vines with surrounding soil
- Do not replant in same spot for 2–3 years
- Apply organic mulch around vine base to prevent soil splash
- Phytosanitation — clean tools with 1% bleach between vines

---

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Coleoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Small round holes bored into pepper berries
- Grubs inside berries — berries turn black and fall prematurely
- Infested berries are hollow and shriveled
- Berries drop well before maturity causing severe yield loss
- Adult beetles are tiny metallic blue-black, visible on spikes

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Season: September–November when berries are forming
- Humid conditions in dense pepper gardens
- Gardens with shady and humid microclimate

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Quinalphos 25% EC — 2 ml/litre spray on spikes and berries
- Application method: Spray on berry clusters at 15-day intervals during September–November

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre spray
- Kaolin particle film spray creates physical barrier

**Prevention:**
- Collect and destroy fallen infested berries daily
- Prune to reduce canopy density and improve air circulation
- Timely harvest to reduce exposure period

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Dark brown to black lesions on stems causing dieback from tip downward
- Circular water-soaked spots on leaves turning brown with yellow halo
- Infected berries show sunken dark spots and fall prematurely
- New shoot tips die back — stag-head appearance on branches
- Salmon-colored spore masses on wet lesions

**Triggering Conditions:**
- High humidity and frequent rain
- Season: Monsoon July–September
- Wounds from pruning, insects, or mechanical damage are entry points

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Carbendazim 50% WP — 1 g/litre spray on affected parts
- Alternatively: Propiconazole 25% EC — 1 ml/litre

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper oxychloride 3 g/litre spray
- Trichoderma viride soil application near vine base

**Prevention:**
- Prune with sterilized tools — dip in 1% bleach between cuts
- Apply Bordeaux paste on pruning cuts
- Maintain vigor through balanced nutrition — potassium is critical for disease resistance

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide

chunks.title: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Black Pepper Pest and Disease Guide
Mosaic Virus (Pepper Veinal Mottle Virus — PVMV)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Viral

**Symptoms:**
- Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves
- Leaves are smaller, distorted, and puckered
- Yellow vein banding visible on leaves held to light
- Stunted growth; reduced flowering and berry set
- No cure once infected

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Transmitted by aphids (Myzus persicae, Aphis gossypii)
- Any season when aphid populations are high
- New plantings near infected older gardens are at high risk

**Treatment:**
- No treatment — remove and destroy infected vines
- Control aphid vector with Imidacloprid 17.8% SL — 0.5 ml/litre

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil spray to reduce aphid vector population
- Reflective silver mulch disorients aphids

**Prevention:**
- Use certified virus-free planting material (rooted cuttings)
- Control aphid vectors in young gardens
- Do not plant near old infected gardens
- Roguing — immediately remove any mosaic-symptomatic vine

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Rice is India's most important staple crop, grown across Tamil Nadu, Punjab, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh, and Kerala. It is susceptible to a wide range of fungal diseases, bacterial infections, and insect pests. Humid tropical conditions during Kharif season create ideal environments for disease outbreaks. Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is strongly recommended to reduce chemical dependency.

---

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Diamond-shaped or spindle-shaped gray lesions with brown borders on leaves (leaf blast)
- Neck of panicle turns black and rots — entire panicle collapses (neck blast — most damaging)
- Nodes turn black and break (node blast)
- Severely affected fields appear burned from a distance

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 24–28°C
- Humidity: Above 80%; leaf wetness for 10+ hours
- Season: Kharif — peak during July–September
- Excessive nitrogen fertilization increases susceptibility dramatically
- Low silicon soils are more vulnerable

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Tricyclazole 75% WP — 0.6 g/litre, spray at first symptom and repeat at 10 days
- Alternatively: Isoprothiolane 40% EC — 1.5 ml/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray — critical to spray at panicle initiation stage as preventive

**Organic Alternative:**
- Silicon spray (potassium silicate 2 g/litre) strengthens cell walls
- Pseudomonas fluorescens 10 g/litre as seed treatment and foliar spray

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Use resistant varieties (IR-64, Swarna, BPT-5204, Pusa Basmati varieties with resistance)
- Avoid excess nitrogen — split application reduces blast risk
- Apply silicon fertilizer (slag) in blast-prone areas
- Maintain optimum plant spacing for air circulation
- Drain field periodically to reduce leaf wetness

---

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Hemiptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Circular patches of dried brown plants in field (hopperburn)
- Yellow to brown discoloration spreading outward from center
- Insects visible at base of plant tillers — tiny brown or white bugs
- Honeydew on leaf sheaths causing sooty mold
- Severe infestation causes complete crop collapse within days

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–30°C
- Humidity: High — continuous flooding and dense canopy favor outbreak
- Season: Kharif — August to October peak
- Excessive nitrogen and close spacing create perfect microclimate
- Insecticide resistance is widespread — rotate chemicals

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Buprofezin 25% SC — 1 ml/litre (targets nymphs)
- Alternatively: Dinotefuran 20% SG — 0.5 g/litre; drain field before spraying at base of plant
- Application method: Direct spray at base of tillers after partial field drainage

**Organic Alternative:**
- Drain field for 3–4 days to expose and kill nymphs
- Release Cyrtorhinus lividipennis (natural predator) — 500 per acre
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre spray at base of plants

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Use BPH-resistant varieties (Swarna Sub-1, IR-64, PTB-33)
- Avoid excess nitrogen in single dose — split into 3 applications
- Maintain proper spacing — do not crowd plants
- Install light traps to monitor adult migration
- Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural enemies

---

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Oval or irregular greenish-gray lesions on leaf sheaths near water level
- Lesions have brown margins and grayish-white centers
- Disease moves up the plant from sheath to flag leaf
- Severely affected tillers die before heading (whiteheads)
- Sclerotia (small brown pellets) visible in infected tissue

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 28–32°C
- Humidity: Very high — standing water and dense canopy
- Season: Kharif — July to September
- High nitrogen and dense planting dramatically increase incidence
- Sclerotia survive in soil and float to spread between plants

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Hexaconazole 5% EC — 2 ml/litre; spray at sheath level
- Alternatively: Propiconazole 25% EC — 1 ml/litre
- Application method: Direct spray at stem base and sheath — not on upper canopy

**Organic Alternative:**
- Trichoderma viride (bioagent) — 2.5 kg/ha soil application at transplanting
- Pseudomonas fluorescens foliar spray — 10 g/litre

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Reduce seed rate — avoid overcrowding
- Split nitrogen into 3 doses; avoid excess at tillering
- Remove and burn infected crop debris after harvest
- Deep plowing in summer to bury sclerotia
- Balanced potassium application improves resistance

---

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Bacterial

**Symptoms:**
- Water-soaked to yellow stripe along leaf margins starting from tip
- Stripe turns pale yellow to white and dries up
- Leaf edges become wavy and dry
- Bacterial ooze visible in early morning on cut leaf ends (milky droplets)
- Entire leaf wilts and dries in severe Kresek phase (seedling death)

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–34°C
- Humidity: High; heavy rain and wind spread bacteria
- Season: Kharif — peak after cyclonic storms or heavy rain
- Flood irrigation spreads bacteria between fields
- Wounds from insects or wind allow entry

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Copper oxychloride 50% WP — 3 g/litre spray
- Alternatively: Streptomycin sulfate + Tetracycline (100 ppm) — use only once
- Application method: Foliar spray covering all leaves; drain field if possible

**Organic Alternative:**
- No effective organic cure — prevention is primary strategy
- Drain fields during outbreak to reduce bacterial spread

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Use resistant varieties (IR-20, Pusa Basmati-1121, Improved Samba Mahsuri)
- Avoid flood irrigation during outbreak — use furrow or drip
- Do not apply excess nitrogen when disease appears
- Remove and destroy severely infected plants
- Avoid movement through wet fields during infection

---

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Stem Borer (Scirpophaga incertulas — Yellow Stem Borer)
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Lepidoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Dead heart — central tiller dies at vegetative stage (yellow and pulls out easily)
- White ear — panicle does not fill grain, turns white at reproductive stage
- Circular holes on leaf sheaths where larvae entered
- Frass inside hollow tiller
- Moths are white with a single black dot — visible at night around light traps

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–30°C
- Season: Active throughout Kharif — peaks at tillering and heading
- Dense canopy and high nitrogen favor population
- Ratoon crop and continuous cropping build up population

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC (Coragen) — 0.3 ml/litre spray or granule
- Alternatively: Cartap hydrochloride 4G — 18 kg/ha granule applied in standing water
- Application method: Spray on foliage at egg hatching stage; granules in water

**Organic Alternative:**
- Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray — 2 g/litre at egg hatching
- Trichogramma japonicum egg parasitoid — release 5 cards per acre at each brood
- Light traps to catch and kill adult moths

chunks.title: Rice Pest and Disease Guide
heading_path: Stem Borer (Scirpophaga incertulas — Yellow Stem Borer)
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Remove ratoon crop immediately after main crop harvest
- Clip leaf tips during transplanting to remove egg masses
- Install light traps — 1 per acre from 15 days after transplanting
- Avoid close spacing; maintain good air circulation
- Balance nitrogen — excess nitrogen attracts egg-laying females

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Tomato is one of the most economically important vegetables in India, grown across Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu. It is highly susceptible to fungal diseases, viral infections, and insect pests — especially under humid conditions or poor crop rotation. Early identification and prompt action are critical to protect yield and quality.

---

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Dark brown to black circular spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older leaves
- Yellow halo surrounding the spots
- Leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
- Stem lesions at soil level in seedlings (damping-off)
- Dark sunken spots on fruit near stem end

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 24–29°C
- Humidity: Above 70%
- Season: Most common in Kharif (September–October) and post-monsoon
- Favored by alternating wet and dry periods

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Mancozeb 75% WP — 2 g/litre, spray every 7–10 days
- Alternatively: Chlorothalonil 75% WP — 2 g/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray covering both leaf surfaces

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper oxychloride 50% WP — 3 g/litre spray
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre + soap 1 ml/litre as spreader

**Prevention:**
- Use certified disease-free seed
- Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip
- Remove and destroy infected plant debris
- Maintain crop rotation — avoid tomato after tomato or potato
- Apply mulch to reduce soil splash on leaves

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Oomycete)

**Symptoms:**
- Water-soaked pale green to brown patches on leaves, rapidly enlarging
- White cottony growth on underside of leaves during humid mornings
- Dark brown lesions on stems
- Fruit shows firm brown patches that spread quickly
- Entire field can collapse within 7–10 days in cool humid weather

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 10–20°C (cool conditions)
- Humidity: Above 90%
- Season: October–December in Nashik and Deccan; December–February in hills
- Spreads very fast during foggy or rainy spells

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) — 2.5 g/litre, spray every 5–7 days
- Alternatively: Cymoxanil + Mancozeb — 2 g/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray — begin preventively before disease appears in high-risk weather

**Organic Alternative:**
- Copper hydroxide (Kocide) — 3 g/litre
- Bordeaux mixture 1% — spray fortnightly as preventive

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Plant resistant varieties (Arka Rakshak, Pusa Hybrid-1)
- Avoid planting in low-lying areas with poor drainage
- Destroy volunteer tomato plants
- Monitor weather forecasts — spray before rain forecast
- Space plants adequately for air circulation

---

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Viral

**Symptoms:**
- Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves
- Leaves are malformed, smaller than normal, curled inward
- Stunted plant growth
- Fruits show yellow blotches or internal browning
- No treatment possible once infected

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Transmitted by contact (tools, hands) and seed
- Not transmitted by insects (unlike other viruses)
- Any season; worse in dense plantings

**Treatment:**
- No cure — infected plants must be removed and destroyed immediately
- Disinfect tools with 1% sodium hypochlorite between plants

**Organic Alternative:**
- No organic treatment — prevention only

**Prevention:**
- Use certified virus-free seed or treated seed
- Wash hands before handling plants
- Do not use tobacco near tomato plants
- Remove and burn infected plants immediately
- Avoid replanting tomato in infected field for 2 seasons

---

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect

**Symptoms:**
- Tiny white flying insects on underside of leaves
- Leaves turn yellow, curl upward
- Sticky honeydew on leaf surface causing sooty mold (black coating)
- Whitefly is the primary vector for Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
- Severe infestations cause plant wilting and fruit drop

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C
- Humidity: Low to moderate (dry conditions favor population buildup)
- Season: Kharif and Rabi both; peak in November–January
- Dense planting and dust on leaves worsen infestation

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL — 0.5 ml/litre, spray on leaf undersides
- Alternatively: Thiamethoxam 25% WG — 0.3 g/litre
- Application method: Direct spray on leaf undersides; rotate insecticides to prevent resistance

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre + garlic extract 5 ml/litre spray
- Yellow sticky traps — 10 per acre to monitor and reduce population
- Reflective silver mulch repels whitefly

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Install 40-mesh nylon net in nursery
- Remove heavily infested leaves and destroy
- Plant marigold border rows as trap crop
- Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization which attracts whitefly
- Monitor weekly with yellow sticky traps

---

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Lepidoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Small circular entry holes on fruit surface
- Caterpillar found inside fruit feeding on seeds and pulp
- Frass (excrement) visible around entry hole
- Damaged fruits rot quickly due to secondary infection
- Young larvae initially feed on leaves and flowers before entering fruit

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 25–35°C
- Season: Active throughout growing season; peak in September–November
- High nitrogen fertilization increases susceptibility
- Continuous tomato cultivation increases moth population

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Spinosad 45% SC — 0.3 ml/litre spray on fruits and foliage
- Alternatively: Chlorantraniliprole (Coragen) — 0.3 ml/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray covering fruits thoroughly; spray in evening

**Organic Alternative:**
- Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) — 2 g/litre spray at egg hatching stage
- Neem seed kernel extract (NSKE) 5% spray
- Pheromone traps — 5 traps per acre to monitor adult moth

chunks.title: Tomato Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Install pheromone traps from transplanting onwards
- Remove and destroy damaged fruits immediately
- Deep summer plowing to kill pupae in soil
- Avoid mono-cropping of tomato for multiple seasons
- Intercrop with coriander or basil to attract natural enemies

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
Wheat is the primary Rabi crop across Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and Madhya Pradesh. The crop faces serious threats from rust diseases, smut, and insect pests. Punjab and Haryana are considered the wheat bowl of India — disease outbreaks here have national food security implications. Early season monitoring from January onwards is critical.

---

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Yellow to orange pustules arranged in stripes along leaf veins
- Powdery yellow-orange spores rub off on fingers
- Leaves turn yellow then brown and dry
- Early infection at flag leaf stage is most damaging
- Spreads rapidly in cool weather — field can be fully infected in 10 days

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 10–15°C (cool weather is critical trigger)
- Humidity: High; dew and foggy mornings
- Season: January–March in Punjab, December–February in hills
- New virulent races (78S84, 46S119) overcome resistant varieties

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Propiconazole 25% EC — 1 ml/litre, spray immediately at first sign
- Alternatively: Tebuconazole 25.9% EC — 1 ml/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray; repeat after 10–14 days if conditions persist

**Organic Alternative:**
- No effective organic treatment — act fast with chemical
- Sulfur dust 25 kg/ha can slow spread slightly

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Use rust-resistant varieties (HD-3086, WB02, PBW-343 with Yr genes)
- Monitor fields weekly from January in Punjab
- Avoid late sowing which extends crop duration into rust season
- Do not over-irrigate — wet canopy favors spore germination

---

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal

**Symptoms:**
- Small round orange-brown pustules scattered randomly on leaves (not in stripes)
- Brown powdery spores rub off on touch
- Severely affected leaves dry and die
- Usually appears after yellow rust season — February to March
- Less damaging than yellow rust but can cause 20–30% yield loss

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 15–22°C (warmer than yellow rust)
- Humidity: Moderate to high
- Season: February–March peak in plains

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Propiconazole 25% EC — 1 ml/litre spray
- Application method: Foliar spray at first pustule appearance

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre has limited effectiveness — use only for very mild infections

**Prevention:**
- Same as yellow rust — resistant varieties and timely sowing
- Ensure balanced potassium nutrition which improves rust tolerance

---

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Fungal (Seed-borne)

**Symptoms:**
- Entire ear replaced by black powdery spore mass
- Black smutted heads visible above healthy crop at heading
- Spore mass disperses rapidly at flowering — infects neighboring plants
- Affected plants are taller and head earlier than healthy plants
- No grain formed in infected heads

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Spread during flowering — wind-borne spores infect open flowers
- Cool, humid conditions at flowering favor spore germination
- Seed-borne — infected seed carries the fungus inside the embryo

**Treatment:**
- No field treatment possible — disease is inside the plant
- Rogue out and destroy infected heads before spores disperse (bag and remove)

**Organic Alternative:**
- Hot water seed treatment — soak seed in hot water at 52°C for 10 minutes then dry

**Prevention:**
- Use certified disease-free seed
- Seed treatment with Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% (Vitavax) — 2.5 g/kg seed
- Biproxy (Tebuconazole 2% DS) seed treatment — 1.5 g/kg seed
- Do not save seed from fields with smut infection

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Hemiptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Clusters of small green to brown soft-bodied insects on leaves, stem, and ear
- Sticky honeydew on plant surface causing sooty mold
- Leaves turn yellow; severe infestations cause plant wilting
- Aphids on ears reduce grain filling — shriveled grain
- Economic threshold: 50 aphids per tiller at tillering; 200 per ear at heading

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Temperature: 15–25°C
- Season: December–March in Rabi wheat
- Dry weather and warm spells in February–March cause rapid population buildup
- Excess nitrogen fertilization attracts aphids

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL — 0.5 ml/litre spray
- Alternatively: Dimethoate 30% EC (Rogor) — 2 ml/litre
- Application method: Foliar spray; treat only when threshold exceeded to protect natural enemies

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem oil 5 ml/litre + soap 1 ml/litre
- Spray water jet to dislodge colonies
- Protect natural enemies — ladybirds, lacewings, parasitic wasps

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Prevention:**
- Timely sowing (November 1–15) to avoid peak aphid season
- Balanced fertilization — avoid excess nitrogen
- Do not over-irrigate — high humidity favors aphids
- Monitor weekly from December onwards

---

chunks.title: Wheat Pest and Disease Guide
chunks.text:
**Type:** Insect (Isoptera)

**Symptoms:**
- Plants wilt suddenly and pull out easily from soil
- Root and stem base eaten — hollowed out with mud-filled galleries
- Affected plants appear in patches in the field
- More common in sandy soils and fields with undecomposed organic matter
- Problem worsens in dry soils

**Triggering Conditions:**
- Dry soil conditions — termites avoid waterlogged soil
- Sandy soils with low clay content
- Fields with crop stubble or roots left undecomposed
- Season: Most active in dry November–December after sowing

**Treatment:**
- Primary chemical: Chlorpyrifos 20% EC — 2.5 litre/ha dissolved in irrigation water (drench)
- Alternatively: Imidacloprid 600 FS seed treatment — 6 ml/kg seed before sowing

**Organic Alternative:**
- Neem cake 250 kg/ha incorporated in soil before sowing
- Bacillus cereus biocontrol application

**Prevention:**
- Deep summer plowing to destroy termite colonies
- Remove all crop stubble and roots before sowing
- Avoid applying raw fresh manure or green material — use well-composted FYM only
- Seed treatment with chlorpyrifos or imidacloprid before sowing
